data.title: Tesla's 'iPhone' Moment Is Here
data.description:
I discussed why I increased my Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) position around the lows in March and April, as well as on the massive early June drop when CEO Elon Musk's Twitter storm erupted, which included the now infamous and since deleted "Jeffrey Epstein Tweet."

Now, you might be wondering why I am accumulating Tesla's stock when the company's CEO exhibits what could arguably be characterized as erratic behavior.

Well, generally, I feel that Tesla's stock hit a long-term bottom around the $220-200 support range, and due to numerous constructive fundamental factors like its market leading position in EVs, full self-driving ("FSD"), robotaxis, AI, robotics, energy, and more, Tesla's stock could have enormous upside ahead.

Do you remember when all those Teslas were burning? Well, due to the ultra-bearish sentiment and other transitory factors, that unprecedented chaotic period very likely coincided with the bottom in Tesla's stock, in my opinion.

Now, technically, Tesla's stock is in a very interesting place, as it appears to be forming a long term W-shaped bottom. We can also view this image as a bullish long term inverse head and shoulders set-up. Moreover, we have the 50-day MA crossing over the 200-day MA recently. This dynamic illustrates a bullish golden-cross, which is also suggestive of a long term bottoming process, which supports the likelihood of higher price action, especially in the intermediate and long term.

While Tesla's stock could pull back to about the $300-280 support range, it is not likely to fall beyond that unless we have a considerable broad market correction. In this "bearish case scenario," we could witness Tesla's stock test around the $270-250 support range.

Tesla's Q2 Earnings Beat Estimates

Tesla reported revenue of $22.49B, beating the consensus figure by $360M. Normalized EPS came in at $0.40, which was inline with the consensus estimate. My estimate was for $22.65B in sales and adjusted EPS of $0.44.

While Tesla's automotive segment produced about the sales I anticipated, yet its operating costs were a bit higher than I thought. For instance, I thought that Tesla's automotive sales revenues would be about $15.6B (without tax credits), and Tesla delivered about $15.8B. The company also utilized less tax credit revenue than I anticipated, $439M vs. my estimate of $700M, which is a positive sign.

This dynamic may illustrate that Tesla is slowly winding down its partial dependence on tax credit revenues. Therefore, it should remain profitable once the EV tax credits are phased out, and it could increase its profitability by generating more revenues from higher margin businesses in the coming years.

Also, I believe that Tesla could regain more momentum auto sales wise, and could return to considerable growth in its automotive segment early next year. Moreover, Tesla reaffirmed that it's on track to introduce its new lower cost EV in H2 2025. The new vehicle could contribute considerable revenues to Tesla's top line next year, enabling it to surpass future revenues and ESP estimates.

It's Not All About Selling Cars Anymore

Services and other revenues expanded nicely to about $3.05B, about a 16% QoQ rise. This was above my $2.9B estimate. However, Tesla's energy generation and storage "EG&S" business' sales only rose slightly over its previous quarter.

The EG&S segment brought in about $2.79B in sales, slightly lower than my $3B estimate. Due to the White House's harsh stance on solar and renewable energy in general (aside from nuclear), it's a challenging time for the entire alt-energy industry now.

Therefore, I am not surprised that Tesla's EG&S business isn't growing as fast now as some would like. Nonetheless, Tesla's energy segment has enormous potential going forward, and this business alone could be worth around $200B.

Tesla's Profitability Better Than Expected

Tesla produced a gross margin of 17.2% in Q2, which was a considerable improvement over last Q's 16.3%, and slightly above my 17% estimate. If we exclude the tax credit revenue Tesla's gross margin was 15.6%, still relatively high, especially for a "car company."

For instance, Ford's (F) recent gross margins have been around 8%, while GM's (GM) roughly 12%. Moreover, Tesla's automotive sales segment gross margin (not including tax credits) was 17.2%, illustrating superior manufacturing efficiency over its competitors. In addition, Tesla also posted a $284M gain on its Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holdings, which atypical for a car company.

It's All About The Ecosystem And The Future Now

People often ask me "what is Tesla's FSD advantage?" "Why should it be the one to succeed and potentially 'corner the robotaxi market' in the future, and why not Waymo, or someone else?"

Well, it's because no-one has the ecosystem or the technological advantage that Tesla does, in my view, not even Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), when it comes to self-driving cars.

First, Tesla uses its own proprietary technology, and not lidar detectors like most other companies utilize. While the system is far from perfect yet, I believe Tesla will iron out the kinks in time.

Tesla can utilize higher resolution cameras, improve neuralink capabilities, incorporate additional sensors, improve AI capabilities, and much more. Knowing Tesla, there is a very high probability that the company will be able to figure out solutions to its transitory FSD issues.

Therefore, it could become the number one player in this ultra-lucrative and highly profitable robotaxi/FSD space. Even Goldman Sachs thinks that Tesla has a significant "leg up," so I am not the only one.

Here's How I View Tesla And It's Not A "Car Stock"

Think about it this way. Each Tesla vehicle is actually a robot on wheels. There could be about 5 million active Tesla vehicles globally now, and each one of them has numerous cameras. These cameras continuously collect and transmit data back to Tesla's enormous data center AI system, and its supercomputer Dojo.

Tesla is also building thousands of robots this year, and it may produce around 50,000 units next year. I don't know about you, but I view this as a "iPhone" moment, similar to the one I had in 2006. When I saw Apple coming out with a superior, market-leading revolutionary product (the iPhone), I just knew that in several years essentially everyone would have one. I bought Apple's stock for the long term in 2006, and the rest is history.

I have a similar feeling regarding Tesla's Optimus robot. It seems so much more refined than anything the competition has to offer, in my view. Moreover, it looks amazing in my opinion, as many "competing" robots have wires and other aesthetic issues. In other words, the Optimus bot reminds me very much of the iPhone in 2006.

Robots Could Be A Massive Business For Tesla

I understand that the robot is a much more expensive product, but that doesn't mean that between now and 2030 many families and business won't own them. If Tesla sells only about 100,000 bots in a year for $25,000 each it would equate to about $2.5B in revenues.

Elon Musk believes that the humanoid robot segment will grow into a multi-trillion-dollar business, and Tesla could dominate this sector as well. While Elon has been a little optimistic about some timelines, I've learned not to doubt the man too much, especially when it comes to highly capable and applicable future technologies, like robots.

Tesla aims to produce about 1M robots a year by 2030. Therefore, if we approximate an average selling price of $25K for each robot, this segment could bring in an additional $25B annually for Tesla's top line.

Short Selling Risks To Consider

While I have nothing against shorting stocks for short-term profits, Tesla is not a stock I would short in the intermediate and long term. I've never thought it a wise idea to short companies that are involved in developing and implementing revolutionary products and technologies.

So, I just think that anyone who is shorting Tesla longer term with put options or any other instruments because of its seemingly high valuation could be making a mistake. I only mention this because I don't want to see more people lose even more money betting against Tesla, as I've been witnessing this dynamic for over ten years now.

Where Tesla's Stock Could Go Longer Term

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue (in billions) $112 $138 $173 $220 $270 $325
Revenue growth 15% 23% 25% 27% 23% 20%
EPS $2.50 $4.70 $6.25 $9.70 $14 $19
EPS growth 3.3% 88% 33% 55% 44% 36%
Forward P/E

110

 100 95 85 75 65
Stock price $525 $625 $920 $1,190

$1,425

 $1,625

Source: The Financial Prophet.

I am keeping my intermediate and longer term estimates the same as in my previous analysis. I see no need to change them at this point. However, I could adjust estimates higher as more clarity materializes concerning Tesla's new vehicle, robotaxi and FSD magnetization growth, and potential improvements in general efficiencies and automotive sales.

Therefore, these are my lower/base case scenario projections. In a more bullish outcome, Tesla could achieve market dominance in FSD, robotaxis, EVs, robotics, successfully integrating many of the moving parts into its highly advanced ecosystem. In such a scenario, and I believe it is highly plausible, Tesla's stock could go much higher, and my 2030 price target range remains $2,500-3,000 for Tesla's stock.

Risks To My Bullish Thesis

Of course, there are many risks to the bull case in Tesla, which could be why there are so many bearish views about the stock. Tesla faces increased competition in the EV space, and its sales have slumped due to political involvement by CEO Elon Musk.

Also, there is the risk from the current administration possibly pressuring Tesla because of the ruptured relationship between President Trump and Elon Musk. The EV tax credits are being phased out, and there is still plenty of uncertainty regarding Tesla's Cybertaxi program and the safety and full-scale functionality of its FSD. The risks as well as the potential rewards are abundant, and investors should examine these and other risks carefully before purchasing Tesla's stock.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Buffett Drops The Mic
data.description:
Introduction

Buffett's 60 years at the helm of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) is coming to an end. His performance is staggering: a 19.9% average annual return over 60 years (up to the 2024 year-end). If you calculate the performance until the annual meeting on May 3rd, when he announced that Greg Abel will succeed him, the yearly return is 20.2%! Although I believe Buffett is the greatest investor of all time, I think it is wise of him to step down at the top and let the next generation take over. At 94 years old, it's no shame to admit that your best days are behind you. And who knows, maybe Warren will be managing his personal portfolio full time when he retires due to his love for the sport. Actually, that's probably the most likely scenario.

This is a rundown of Berkshire Hathaway's long-term performance, updated annually. I review the figures in the same way that Buffett has historically and provide a valuation framework for Berkshire Hathaway. Without further ado, let's dig into the numbers.

Yardsticks

Berkshire can be viewed from a few different lenses. A fairly simplistic one is to think of Berkshire as having two separate segments of value.

The first is investments in stocks, bonds and cash equivalents which stood at $574 billion at year-end ($488 billion last year) and $581 billion at the end of Q1 2025. Float has funded about $173 billion of these investments.

The second source of value comes from Berkshire's over 60 non-insurance businesses. In their early days, Charlie and Warren focused on increasing the value of the investment portfolio (per-share investment). However, since the late 1980s, they focused more on developing earnings from non-insurance operations (per-share pre-tax earnings). You can also call this bucket operating company earnings. Earnings from insurance companies and investment income are not included in this area as they are implicitly included in the first bucket.

This shift in focus on operating companies accelerated the growth rate of this bucket, while the growth rate of the investment bucket slowed. However, from 2007 onwards, both buckets have slowed down considerably.

This can be seen from the following tables:

Year Per-share investments Years

Compounded annual gain in per-share investments
1965 4
1979 577 1965-1979 42.8%
1993 13,961 1979-1993 25.6%
2007 90,343 1993-2007 14.3%
2021 303,563 2007-2021 9.0%
2024 399,240 2021-2024 9.6%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2007 shareholder letter and financial statements

Per-share investments have increased by 21.1% compounded annually over the entire period from 1965 to 2024 (60 years for those who are counting).

Year Per-share pre-tax earnings Years Compounded annual gain in per-share pre-tax earnings
1965 4
1979 18 1965-1979 11.1%
1993 212 1979-1993 19.1%
2007 4,093 1993-2007 23.5%
2021 17,696 2007-2021 11.0%
2024 19,271 2021-2024 2.8%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2007 shareholder letter and financial statements

Per-share pre-tax earnings have increased by 15.2% compounded annually for the entire period 1965-2024.

The growth rate of non-insurance earnings increased for each period until 2007 but has since then decreased meaningfully, while the growth rate for per-share investments has decreased each whole period. Buffett has been eager to point out that this is what will happen when the base of assets and earnings gets too large. To wit (from his 2007 shareholder letter):

Though these tables may help you gain historical perspective and be useful in valuation, they are completely misleading in predicting future possibilities. Berkshire's past record can't be duplicated or even approached. Our base of assets and earnings is now far too large for us to make outsized gains in the future.

Buffett reminded investors of the same dynamics in his shareholder letter from 2023 (emphasis added by author):

There remain only a handful of companies in this country capable of truly moving the needle at Berkshire, and they have been endlessly picked over by us and by others. Some we can value; some we can’t. And, if we can, they have to be attractively priced. Outside the U.S., there are essentially no candidates that are meaningful options for capital deployment at Berkshire. All in all, we have no possibility of eye-popping performance.

Excluding the earnings from the insurance operations, earnings per share increased by only 2.2% in 2024. Only BHE (energy) experienced growth during the period, while the growth rates of the other operating companies remained flat.

It's really tough for Berkshire to achieve "eye-popping" performance with their existing businesses. Due to the mature nature of the railroad (BNSF) and energy markets, it's reasonable to assume slow growth in the future (excluding add-on acquisitions). In my article from last year, I concluded that Berkshire needs the excellent capital allocation skills of Buffett and his investment managers to outperform the S&P 500. Now that Buffett has announced he is stepping down as CEO by the end of the year, it falls to Greg Abel to gain new insights on where to allocate capital. Hopefully he's the man for the job. Otherwise, I find it very unlikely that Berkshire could beat the market in the long run.

The two sources of value can be further divided into four segments: Insurance, BNSF, BHE, as well as manufacturing, service, and retail.

Insurance

Insurance underwriting had an even better year than last year's outstanding performance, with after-tax profits of $9.0 billion (compared to $5.4 billion in 2023), corresponding to an average cost of float of less than zero. As long as the cost of float is lower than the alternative cost of funding, the insurance underwriting operations are performing well.

Over the last 20 years, Berkshire has had a lower cost of funding compared to, for example, the 10-year Treasury yield at year-end. Even more impressive is the fact that in 18 of these 20 years, Berkshire's cost of float has been below zero.

The funds from insurance have been cost-free more often than not, going back to when Berkshire first entered the insurance business in 1967 with the purchase of National Indemnity.

Year Underwriting earnings ($ millions) Average float ($ millions) Cost of float Year-end yield on 10-year treasuries
2016 1,370 89,500 less than zero 2.4%
2017 -2,219 102,500 2.2% 2.5%
2018 1,566 118,500 less than zero 2.7%
2019 325 126,000 less than zero 1.8%
2020 657 133,500 less than zero 0.9%
2021 870 142,500 less than zero 1.5%
2022 -30 155,500 0.1% 3.9%
2023 5,428 166,500 less than zero 3.9%
2024 9,021 170,000 less than zero 4.6%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

GEICO has been on a strong run during 2023-2024 and is still going strong in 2025. BH Primary had a good year with an underwriting profit of $855 million, although 2025 started with a loss. BHRG had an excellent year (again) with underwriting profits of $2,738 million, although 2025 started with a loss for BHRG as well.

I believe that the year 2024 was the top for Berkshire's insurance earnings, at least for a couple of years, as there is evidence that the insurance P&C market is turning soft in 2025. If Berkshire is to grow its earnings in 2025 and beyond, it must come from its non-insurance businesses.

Pre-tax earnings ($ million)

 GEICO BH Primary BHRG
2016 462 657 1,012
2017 -310 719 -3,648
2018 2,449 670 -1,109
2019 1,506 383 -1,472
2020 3,428 110 -2,700
2021 1,259 607 -755
2022 -1,880 393 1,465
2023 3,635 1,374 1,904
2024 7,813 855 2,738

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

The float has been used for investments in equity and fixed-income securities, which in turn produce investment income. Pre-tax investment income increased to $16.7 billion (~45% growth year-over-year).

The increase stems from interest income, which increased ~90% from the prior year due to still-high interest rates. Dividends declined by ~5% due to Berkshire being a net seller of listed equities.

Berkshire owned about $286 billion of T-bills at the end of 2024 and increased its holdings to $306 billion at the end of Q1 2025. The 1-year treasury rate has decreased compared to last year and is now about 4%. The running yield on the current T-bills is about $12.2 billion.

As I've mentioned numerous times, I hope that Greg Abel and his investment managers make an 'elephant' acquisition i.e. an operating company/listed stock that has the potential to earn 15% on invested capital annually, rather than the 4% that can be earned on T-bills currently.

Non-insurance businesses

This segment comprises all of Berkshire's operating companies that are not included in the insurance segment. The by far largest of these are Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE) and the railroad company Burlington Northern Santa Fe (BNSF).

In addition to these, there's a hodgepodge of other businesses, the largest of which are Marmon (part of the Industrial segment), Clayton Homes (Building products), McLane, Berkshire Hathaway Automotive (Retailing), NetJets, FlightSafety, and TTI (all three part of the Service segment). Pilot is a sizable new wholly owned company as well (part of the energy segment).

BNSF

Year Pre-tax earnings ($ millions)

Tangible net worth ($ millions)

 Pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth Pre-tax earnings on average carrying value
2015 6,775 35,451 - -
2016 5,693 37,793 15.5% 11.1%
2017 6,328 39,289 16.4% 11.9%
2018 6,863 39,754 17.4% 12.6%
2019 7,250 40,284 18.1% 13.2%
2020 6,792 40,378 16.8% 12.3%
2021 7,861 43,258 18.8% 13.9%
2022 7,708 43,678 17.7% 13.2%
2023 6,614 44,270 15.0% 11.2%
2024 6,648 44,153 15.0% 11.2%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

BNSF's asset base remained flat, and the earnings power on this asset base has been around 15-19%. Although earnings growth is slow and highly dependent on GDP, the earnings on average tangible net worth have been fair throughout the period outlined above.

In 2024, pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth remained at 15.0%. As Berkshire bought BNSF at a fairly large premium to net worth, represented by the goodwill item on Berkshire's balance sheet (of which $15.4 billion is allocated to BNSF), the earnings on average carrying value are reduced to 11-14%.

Although BNSF's cash flow from operations is positive, a large chunk of this is allocated to BNSF's maintenance and growth capital expenditures. I believe that the remainder is then sent up to headquarters for reallocation to other profitable opportunities.

BHE

Year After-tax earnings ($ millions) Tangible net worth ($ millions) After-tax earnings on average tangible net worth After-tax earnings on average carrying value
2015 - 31,219 - -
2016 2,507 32,828 7.8% 6.1%
2017 2,351 26,836 7.9% 6.0%
2018 2,924 29,600 10.4% 7.7%
2019 3,144 34,269 9.8% 7.5%
2020 3,544 45,239 8.9% 7.0%
2021 4,446 48,974 9.4% 7.5%
2022 4,775 51,998 9.5% 7.7%
2023 2,962 55,176 5.5% 4.5%
2024 4,163 59,800 7.2% 6.0%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

BHE's yearly capital expenditures are over twice the amount of BNSF's (in the year 2024, BHE spent $9.0 billion on maintenance and expansion capex while BNSF spent $3.7 billion). BHE has a significantly larger asset base than BNSF, although its earnings are smaller.

Due to the nature of the sector in which BHE operates (providing essential services to consumers and customers), the returns on most of its businesses are regulated, i.e., there are no outsized returns to be had here. The flip side of the coin is that BHE can deploy vast amounts of capital at stable and fair returns.

This has been true in the past, but as Warren described in last year's letter and discussed during this year's annual meeting, the regulatory landscape for utilities has changed in certain states, effectively preventing utilities from earning their expected return on equity. In addition, Warren has expressed concern about whether it's worth committing additional capital to Western states that are vulnerable to climate change phenomena, such as wildfires.

The earnings on average tangible net worth increased to 7.2% in 2024. As in BNSF's case, Berkshire bought BHE and its subsidiaries at premiums to net worth, which is why earnings on average carrying value is only 6.0%. The increase in 2024 was due to the wildfire provisions of $1.6 billion in 2023, i.e., estimated damages that potentially have to be paid in the future.

In BHE's case, I don't believe any of the excess cash can be sent to Berkshire for reallocation as the business is so capital-intensive. It actually seems to consume everything it generates and more, as after-tax earnings in 2024 were less than half of the capital expenditures ($4 billion in earnings versus capital expenditures of over $9 billion). The amount of cash needed is staggering, and as the regulatory environment remains uncertain, BHE could face significant headwinds in the years to come, including increased wildfire-related claims and lower returns on equity.

Manufacturing, Service and Retail

Year Pre-tax earnings ($ millions) Tangible net worth ($ millions) Pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth Pre-tax earnings on average carrying value
2016 10,224 54,216 - -
2017 10,927 58,879 19.3% 11.3%
2018 12,308 65,535 19.8% 11.9%
2019 12,365 67,321 18.6% 11.5%
2020 10,889 60,605 17.0% 10.8%
2021 14,552 66,796 22.8% 15.2%
2022 16,219 65,359 24.5% 16.3%
2023 17,589 85,460 23.3% 15.5%
2024 16,843 92,145 19.0% 13.0%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

Combined, this segment is the largest of the non-insurance businesses. However, there are over 60 companies included in this segment, so individually, they're significantly smaller than BNSF and BHE.

These businesses enjoy enviable economics, earning an average of 19.0% on their tangible net worth in 2024. As is the case with BNSF and BHE, Berkshire has acquired these businesses at a substantial premium to net worth, and the pre-tax earnings on average carrying value was 13.0% in 2024. The decrease from the previous years has to due with Pilot's inclusion in the figures. Due to the capital intensive nature of its business model it drags down the returns for the whole group.

Intrinsic business value

Charlie and Warren used to measure how their per-share intrinsic value developed over time in comparison to the S&P 500. Intrinsic value is, however, an elusive concept. A good proxy for it is per-share book value and how it has developed.

Book value obviously understates intrinsic value. The amount by which intrinsic value exceeds book value (in relative terms) doesn't change substantially from year to year, however, which is why per-share book value is a good yardstick for intrinsic value.

Per-share book value increased in 2024 by about 16.6% while BRK's share price increased by 27.1%. If we assume that BRK's share price approximates intrinsic value, it implies that the ratio of intrinsic value to book value increased during 2024. It's, however, a leap to assume that BRK's share price performance during a single year would approximate the change in intrinsic value.

In the long term, however, the share price to per-share book value ratio should resemble the per-share intrinsic value to per-share book value ratio as the share price should converge to per-share intrinsic value (with large swings in the interim). In 2024, the P/B increased from 1.37x to 1.51x. We need, however, a longer-term metric to conclude what the normal premium should be.

Below I have included a graph that shows how P/B has developed over the last 10 years in addition to the 10-year median P/B. The long-term median has remained fairly stable at around 1.40x. The current valuation has risen quite much, ~13% above the long-term trend, indicating overvaluation, although it has decreased from the 10-year peak.

Data by YCharts

I've also updated my valuation of BRK, which I described in more detail here.

My current estimate of Berkshire's intrinsic value is about $1,072 billion. From my year-ago valuation of $912 billion, the value has increased by $151 billion. The most significant changes on the operating side are insurance underwriting (which I don't think is sustainable) and BHE. On the non-operating side, cash and T-bills increased by $180 billion, while listed investments decreased by $79 billion due to Berkshire's net selling of equities. The proceeds were used to buy T-bills. The difference between the increase in cash and the decrease in equities of $101 billion is primarily due to the fact that equities generated positive returns during the year, in addition to the cash generated from other businesses and T-bills.

At BRK's current market capitalization of $1,036 billion, my estimate is 4% higher than the market cap and therefore doesn't fill the requirement of a 20% margin of safety. This is why I rate Berkshire Hathaway a hold at these prices. In addition, assuming insurance underwriting earnings decrease during 2025, it will have a negative impact on the valuation going forward.

Finally, I like to look at how the gains of the equity securities have developed during the period. As we can see, 2024 was another stellar year for Buffett with $53 billion in equity gains. There will be large swings in this segment in the future, and one should consider the cumulative change over time to get a proper picture of Berkshire's investment success. During Buffett's 60-year period, it's just an extraordinary achievement. Zooming in on the cumulative gain since the start of 2018, it looks very decent as well: over $218 billion of cumulative equity gains!

data.title: AGNC: Better Not To Fight Against The Market (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AGNC: Q2's Steep Volatility Was A Timely Reminder

mREIT investors endured a challenging early Q2, as Trump's tariff turmoil wreaked havoc in the market and led to a de-risking posture, which also widened spreads unexpectedly. As a result, it also led a hammering in the stock of AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC), notwithstanding its leadership among its peers, with a market cap of almost $10B.

Accordingly, in price performance terms, AGNC fell more than 25% from its 2025 highs before bottoming out in April. Additionally, while the broad market has taken out new highs, investor sentiments have remained languid in AGNC, as the stock continues to trade below its long-term valuation average while struggling for traction to regain its upward cadence toward its 2025 peak.

Hence, I think it's opportune for me to provide a timely update for AGNC investors on whether they should consider seizing the current initiative to partake in what seems like a highly attractive dividend yield of 15%. Following my AGNC writeup in October 2024, the mREIT has performed reasonably okay, slightly trailing the S&P 500's (SPX) (SPY) with a total return of 7.5%. Hence, the magnitude of its dividend yield has arguably fortified investors from an even more pervasive hammering, as the company recorded a mixed earnings release, imputed to Q2's unanticipated market volatility.

AGNC: Structural Underperformance Likely To Stymie Near-Term Recovery Efforts

While the downtrend bias in AGNC's tangible book value per share (Q2: $7.81) over the past year has arguably hurt investor sentiments, the company's core earnings per share (Q2: $0.38) should still sustain its current capital allocation. The company has also primed its portfolio, anticipating interest rate reductions moving forward. However, due to the decline in its tangible book value per share, it also spurred a reversal in its economic return to -1% in the previous quarter. While it's obviously not a welcomed development, I believe it's too early for us to ascertain whether it represents something more structural or a temporal impact on its portfolio, given the market volatility that afflicted its tangible book value in early Q2.

Furthermore, AGNC management highlighted that it had already regained "about 1%" of its tangible book value per share in July (post-monthly dividend accrual). Hence, unless you expect that the market sentiments could worsen, leading to another fallout over the next few months, I believe it's reasonable for us to contemplate that another fallout might not be the base case.

In addition, while the Q2 market stress impinged on its economic return, the mREIT hasn't faced significant challenges in meeting its dividend payout of $0.36 per share from its Q2 core earnings. Moreover, the company retains the flexibility of deploying the ATM equity proceeds raised at a "significant premium" in Q2 to bolster its forward earnings potential. Also, management assured us that the current leverage (about 7.6x tangible equity) still supports the generation of a "mid-to-high $0.30s to low-to-mid-$0.40s range" moving forward to secure its dividend payouts to income investors.

AGNC's Declining Book Value Per Share Says It All

Nevertheless, the portfolio is still expected to undergo a decline in tangible book value per share through 2026, although it's expected to finish higher than what it recorded in Q2.

Hence, it seems like analysts have maintained their downcast posture on what appears to be a structural downtrend on the company's ability to add long-term shareholder value, consistent with the downward sloping price action seen in its share price in the following chart.

Accordingly, the ongoing equity raise (although priced at a premium to TBVPS) hasn't helped the company overturn the glaring downward spiral examined, as seen in its tangible book value per share trajectory. Hence, I don't think a massive valuation upgrade/re-rating opportunity could be forthcoming, as the market increasingly demands a higher margin of safety for its underperformance.

Buy Or Sell AGNC Stock?

With AGNC's forward yield now at the 15% mark, it's already substantively over its 10Y average of 12.2%. Yet, some could argue that the borrow short (repo) and lend long business model (long-duration Agency MBS assets—with implicit government guarantee) hasn't quite worked as well as anticipated. And the downward trending portfolio value could continue to "eat into" the high dividend yields, widening the discount rate (or dividend yields) that investors require to hold AGNC over time.

Nevertheless, some positive aspects should emerge in the next 12 months. Catalysts such as expectations of further interest rate cuts (lowering its short-term repo borrowing rates) and the continuation of the yield curve inversion should also benefit its long-term asset pricing.

In addition, with the market environment having returned to a less volatile state as Trump goes into deal-making mode with America's principal trading partners, I think the market has come to terms with what is construed as more normalized market conditions. While the ongoing trade talks could still take a turn for the worse, I believe the thesis of "escalate to de-escalate" is increasingly noted in Trump's playbook, plausibly helping to calm the nerves of AGNC investors, previously perturbed with the spreads widening experienced in early April.

Hence, I believe the mREIT should benefit from these near-term catalysts, although I'm much less certain about whether its ability to drive a long-term recovery is still sound. With a persistent downward trend in its tangible book value per share pressuring its ability to outperform through future share issuances, the market is expected to maintain a relatively high dividend yield to offset such potential losses and dilution.

And while the company believes the 7.6x leverage ratio still offers sufficient room to maneuver, the uncertainties in the business environment and tariff-related impacts on the overall economy could persist. Hence, I don't think it's prudent for the company to engage in even higher leverage to juice its portfolio value, with short-term rates potentially also being hampered by the potential tariff pass through.

Therefore, it seems like even mREIT leaders like AGNC might have to contend with a fair amount of ambivalence, suggesting the structural headwinds resisting a more robust recovery could persist for some time to come.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: The Growing Divergence Between The Market And Economy
data.description:
I could not be more pleased with how well the stock market has performed over the past three months. Considering I have minimal exposure to the technology sector, the recent improvement in breadth has helped my portfolios close their performance gap with the tech-dominated S&P 500 index on a year-to-date basis. In fact, my top pick in each S&P 500 sector for 2025, which is a portfolio of 11 stocks I constructed at the beginning of this year, has now produced a double-digit average rate of return. While I have grown increasingly concerned about the macroeconomic outlook and market valuations in my analysis over the past couple of months, I always maintain a core exposure to the stock market. Therefore, I am always looking for reasons to be optimistic, but I see a growing divergence between the market and economy.

As most investors with some experience know, the market is not the economy. The market is a consensus view of how well the economy is performing, which can swing violently in both directions over short periods of time. Things in the real economy don’t change anywhere near as quickly as our views about how the economy is performing. That said, the economy and the market always cross paths eventually, as can be seen in the chart below, because stock prices are a function of corporate profits, and profits are a function of the rate of economic growth. Note how far apart the two are from each other today.

As the S&P 500 keeps climbing to one new all-time high after another, the rate of economic growth has been below trend and looks to be slowing further in the third quarter. Trade policy has created lots of distortions in the GDP figures below, as during the first quarter, there was a surge in imports, which detracted from growth, as well as a surge in inventories, which added to it. That unwound during the second quarter, as imports plunged, increasing GDP, while inventories were drawn down, reducing it. The most important column in the chart below is consumer spending (PCE), which accounts for approximately 70% of GDP. What has worried me in recent months is that spending grew just 0.5% in Q1, contributing just 0.31 to GDP, which is what led to a contraction in economic activity. Furthermore, the estimated rebound in Q2 has steadily declined from spending growth of 3.3% (contributing 2.29% to GDP) to just 1.5%, which equates to a contribution of just 1.01%.

Tomorrow’s personal spending and income report will provide us with another update in this GDPNow estimate from the Atlanta Fed, and my suspicion is that it will be revised lower.

I follow retail gasoline demand as one of my real-time pulses on economic activity, because consumers typically drive to go to work, as well as do just about everything that involves discretionary spending. It is like the blood pumping through the veins of our enormous economy. We drive to restaurants, bars, the grocery store, and the mall. We also drive to vacation or go to the airport. I am concerned that the four-week consumption average has fallen below last year’s level for three consecutive weeks. Last week, that average was 4.8% below year-ago levels, despite gas prices being 10% cheaper than they were last year. Typically, lower gasoline prices encourage more demand, but not this year. This suggests that the rate of consumer spending growth continues to slow in the third quarter.

We need the rate of economic growth to accelerate during the second half of this year to achieve the sequential increase in revenue growth that the consensus expects in Q3 and Q4 of 2025. According to FactSet, the forecast is that year-over-year revenue growth of 4.4% in Q2 will increase to 4.9% in Q3 and 5.4% in Q4.

I see a growing disconnect here, as market prices are rapidly rising as the rate of economic growth is slowing meaningfully. Something has to give way. The more expensive the broad market becomes, the sooner we need to see the high-frequency economic data start to improve. I hope it does. If it does not, we are going to have a rude awakening during the second half of this year.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 229: $22,900 Allocated, $2,412.94 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
It’s been a tale of 2 markets in 2025, and the bull run doesn’t want to stop. At the beginning of the year, if I had told people that the S&P 500 would decline to 4,835.04 in the beginning of April and be hovering around 6,300 3 months later, everyone would have thought I lost my mind. On a YTD basis, the S&P 500 is up roughly 8%, but from the recent lows, the market has rebounded by more than 30%. Sentiment continues to be strong heading into earnings season for the technology industry, and investors are still hopeful that the Fed pivots on monetary policy sooner rather than later. The market continued to move higher this week as the S&P 500 climbed 0.41% while the Nasdaq increased by 0.85%. At the beginning of the year, I had indicated that my bull case was 7,000 on the S&P 500, and the crazy part is that after retracing under the 5,000 level, the market may be in a position to touch 7,000 by the close of 2025 if Q2 and Q3 earnings seasons can show earnings expansion. I’m very excited about the future because I had a long-time horizon to build out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while several sectors were negatively impacted by the higher rate environment and now, I am continuously setting new portfolio records. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio finished week 229 with the largest account value it had, with the most profitability generated and the largest amount of forward dividend income being projected. I am excited for the future, and I can’t wait to see how the rest of this earnings season unfolds.

The market continued to rip higher, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio set 3 new records this week. After allocating $22,900 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 229 at $29,642.70. The account's profitability increased by 0.51% ($34.13) to $6,742.70, which is a return on invested capital of 29.44%. This week, I added to my positions in the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM), and NNN REIT (NNN). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $43.17 of dividend income in week 229. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $13.67 (0.57%) to $2,412.94. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,429.51 of income since inception, which is 19.34% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. In the beginning of this series there were many comments that questioned how this strategy would perform and when I look at the historical data I am pleased with the outcome. The Divided Harvesting Portfolio has mitigated downside risk, generated a profit, and is producing a monthly average of $201.08 in dividend income on a forward basis. I think there is a lot of upside potential for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio and that the amount of dividend income being generated and reinvested will prove to be a hue asset in the future.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The April dip may have been more profound than when the war in Ukraine started. Since the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio’s inception we have seen geopolitical conflicts, macroeconomic headwinds, natural disasters, supply chain issues, and an Administration change, yet the results within the portfolio have been strong. I wanted to document how I would build and income producing portfolio from the ground up on a budget of $100 per week. My intent was to have a portfolio that could mitigate downside risk while generating recurring dividend income. Capital appreciation has been a secondary goal for this portfolio, and I am limiting my single stock exposure to 5% and each sector to 20% of the portfolio. I am excited to see how the rest of the year is going to progress, and I am thrilled that I still have some more time to allocate capital toward REITs, BDCs and financials before the Fed pivots on monetary policy. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at its highest portfolio value since inception with $6,742.70 of profits and is generating $2,412.94 in forward dividend income which is an 8.14% portfolio yield. I am very happy with the progress and believe the best is yet to come.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $791.60 (32.81%)
  • Equities $573.20 (23.76%)
  • CEFs $406.51 (16.85%)
  • REITs $393.64 (16.31%)
  • BDCs $247.99 (10.28%)

investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 29 weeks in 2025, $1,250.13 of dividend income was produced, which is 77.02% of the total income from 2024. There were 415 positions that paid a dividend, which is 58.53% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $43.17 of dividend income generated this week, which kept the amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis at $43.11 in 2025. This is an increase of $11.89, or 38.10% YoY, and there have been 3 individual weeks when $100 of dividend income was exceeded. We’re now in the second half of 2025, and I am excited to see how Q3 plays out. In Q1, I generated $535.63 of dividend income, which increased by 4.9% in Q2 to $561.85. As the dividend income continues to compound and I continue to allocate capital toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, I believe that I could add an additional $75-$100 of dividend income being produced in the back half of 2025.

Last week I generated $43.17 in dividend income, which took July’s total dividend income to $119.63. There are still 2 weeks of dividend income left to be generated, and this could end up being the 2nd time this year when I exceed $200 in monthly dividend income. When I look at the historical trend, it makes me very bullish on how much forward dividend income will be generated. Since the portfolio’s inception, I have generated $4,429.51, which is 19.34% of the total amount of capital I have invested. I think the most exciting part is that this is a strong base, and as I continue to allocate capital toward the portfolio, the dividend snowball effect is going to compound at a quicker rate.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. These positions are expected to add roughly $363.40 of forward dividend income from those shares. I have tried increasing my position in positions that are lagging, but it’s becoming increasingly difficult. I am still focused on getting another 5-10 positions to cross over by the end of the year, which would get me to roughly 50% of the portfolio.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are starting to creep back up to the 20% threshold, while REITs are hovering below 19%. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 38.90% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 23.76% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.10% of the portfolio and generate 76.24% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group is hovering and still represents 4.35% of the portfolio. I can’t believe this, as it's been a year and a half since I added a share to that position. I am very happy that only 3 positions exceed 3% of the portfolio. The top-10 positions changed again as SL Green Realty (SLG) and Pfizer (PFE) fell off while the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST) and QQQI made it back onto the list. I want to start adding to Realty Income (O) again but I want it to decline to under 3% of the portfolio before I do. As time progresses, the larger positions should represent less of the portfolio, which I believe will help mitigate my risk further as I won’t be as top heavy.

PFE and SLG are out, and QQQI and BST are back in the top-10, which changed the dynamics of the table below. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,519.06, and they finished the week with a value of $8,601.79. This is an ROI of $2,082.73 (31.95%). There has been $1,163.57 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.85% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $627.08 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.62%. These positions represent 29.04% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.99% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 229 Additions

This week, I added to my positions in NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF, Rithm Capital Corp., and NNN REIT.

NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF

  • I recently wrote an article about QQQI (can be read here) and outlined my investment thesis
  • QQQI has rebounded well from the bottom and gives me confidence in its strategy
  • QQQI’s top holdings are NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (APPL), which I believe will do well in the second half of 2025
  • QQQI is geared towards technology, which I believe will lead the market higher and provide QQQI with more appreciation, so I wanted to add another share before the Magnificent Seven reported earnings
  • QQQI is yielding over 13% and compounding the distributions can do wonders

Rithm Capital Corp

  • Financials started the earnings season off strong, which makes me believe there are a lot of originations coming down the pipe
  • RITM is still trading at a slight discount to book value while yielding over 8%
  • If the Fed does pivot on monetary policy, I believe that the housing market will become unlocked and RITM will become a large beneficiary as its lines of business will experience enhanced activity

NNN REIT

  • NNN is one of my favorite REITs, as its dividend yield exceeds 5.5%, and NNN has grown the dividend for the past 34 years
  • I don’t own enough of NNN to generate at least 1 share from its annual dividends, so I wanted to add to my position
  • I think that NNN is undervalued and believe that shares will benefit from a lower rate environment as their book value should increase from an increase in asset value, which should push the share price higher

Week 230 Game plan

It’s reader suggestion week, so I will be adding a new position or adding to an existing position that was picked by the readers. So far, I am leaning toward the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO), which has come up in the suggestion section numerous times, but that doesn’t mean it's set in stone. Please leave a comment on ideas I should investigate and allocate capital toward.

Conclusion

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio made new all-time highs as I am up $6,742.70 (29.44%) on the initial capital invested while generating $2,412.94 in forward dividend income. The portfolio has become an ETF as there are 102 positions. I currently have a portfolio yield of 8.14% across these positions and a yield of cost of 10.54%. When I look at the charts below, I feel as if the S-curve is only starting to form in the amount of forward dividend income being generated and that I can end the year generating around $225 in monthly average dividend income. I am happy with the progress, and I believe the portfolio is positioned well for a rate cut or if an unexpected event creates turmoil in the markets for a period of time. The best part about the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is that it has established a track record of mitigating risk during difficult macroeconomic environments. Please leave all your suggestions in the comment section, as I look forward to interacting with everyone.

data.title: Middlesex Water: The Stock Has Entered A Buy Zone
data.description:
Middlesex Water (NASDAQ:MSEX) shares, which have fallen by approximately 55% from their peak price since the end of 2021, have now reached a fairly favorable valuation. The company's business is well-functioning and stable, which makes it an attractive investment at the current valuation. Middlesex Water has been able to provide investors with a better than market average total return for decades, primarily through consistently increasing dividends. The future interest rate cut currently postponed by the FED should also have a favorable impact on Middlesex Water's share price.

Business Overview

Founded in 1896, Middlesex Water Company provides water and wastewater services in the states of New Jersey and Delaware. The company has two business segments: regulated and unregulated water services. Different subsidiaries belong to each segment. The regulated segment provides 93% of the company's revenue. The company has a total of 128,000 customers.

The Middlesex system accounts for the largest share, 67%, of sales. This subdivision operates in eastern Middlesex County, New Jersey, and has 61,000 customers. The Tidewater system, operating in the state of Delaware and with 62,000 customers, has a 24% sales share. All other subdivisions such as Pinelands Water, Utility Service Affiliates, Utility Service Affiliates PA, Southern Shores and White Marsh have marginal sales shares that fall in the 1-3% range.

Latest Developments

In April 2024, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency established new PFAS contaminant level regulations ((MCLs)). These are lower than the New Jersey Department of Environment MCLs currently monitored by the company. Under new regulations, water companies must provide the public with information about the level of PFAS in the drinking water they supply starting in 2027. Water companies have 5 years to implement solutions that should bring PFAS levels down to the levels prescribed in the regulations. Middlesex Water is currently conducting preliminary engineering studies to achieve lower MCL levels in its water systems. Although water companies can receive support from national programs to comply with PFAS standards, the company itself will likely have to bear some of the initial costs of water treatment. In any case, it is likely that the new PFAS regulations will increase the company's operating costs to some extent. MSEX itself has forecast an increase in operating expenses in 2025 and 2026.

In April 2025, the company submitted an application to the New Jersey Board of Public Utilities (NJBPU) for a distribution line improvement fee. As a result, Middlesex Water is expected to generate $1.9 million in additional one-time annual revenue starting in June 2025. Two previous similar applications have previously generated a total of $1.1 million in additional revenue. The amounts mentioned will probably be used, among other things, to meet PFAS regulatory requirements.

In August 2024, Tidewater submitted a request to increase its overall water service rates by 25.66% compared to current rates. Based on this request, Tidewater's annual operating revenues would increase by $10.3 million. In late October, the Delaware Public Service Commission authorized the implementation of a temporary rate increase, which will generate an annual revenue of $2.5 million. Therefore, the said request was only partially granted.

In the most recent news, MSEX filed a request with the NJBPU on June 30 to establish new water rates, which should increase the company's annual revenue by $24.9 million. The request is justified by the approximately 100 million dollar investment made by the company to improve water quality and meet environmental requirements. This added approximately 13% to the company's current revenue of $195.7 million.In the coming months, it will become clear how successful this request will be.

MSEX's strategy also envisages gradual new acquisitions. In this area, Tidewater completed the acquisition of the water utility of the city of Ocean View, Delaware, in April 2025 for $4.6 million. This was a small-scale acquisition, representing only approximately 0.5% of MSEX's total market capitalization.

Consistent Revenue And Profit Growth

Like most water service companies, MSEX has been able to grow its profits year after year. The following table provides an overview of the company's revenue and profit development over the last decade.

Year 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
Total Revenues 126.0 132.9 130.8 138.1 134.6 141.6 143.1 162.4 166.3 191.9
Net Income 20.0 22.7 22.8 32.5 33.9 38.4 36.5 42.4 31.5 44.4

MSEX revenues and profits 2015-2024 in millions of US dollars seekingalpha

MSEX's revenue has grown almost every year since 2015. The only exceptions are 2017 and 2019. The CAGR of revenue over the last 9 years has been 4.78%. Net profit has also grown consistently, with the exceptions of 2021 and 2023. The company's net profit CAGR during the period 2015-2024 has been 9.26%. The fact that profits have grown at a faster pace than revenues indicates effective management of the company. The chart below shows the evolution of MSEX net profit margins over the last decade.

As the chart above indicates, the company's net margin has increased from 16% in 2015 to 22% today. Meanwhile, in 2022, this metric was as high as 28%. Thus, Middlesex Water's sales and profits have grown consistently over the last decade, and the decline in share price since 2021 is not related to the company's fundamentals, but rather to the market's previous overly optimistic valuation. MSEX's gross and net margins are also significantly better than the sector averages.

Dividends

Middlesex Water belongs to the category of dividend kings, having increased its dividend for 52 consecutive years to date. Dividends have played a very important role in MSEX's total return over the past 30 years. The 2 charts below provide an overview of the company's performance over the past 30 years compared to the SP500 index. The top chart represents total return (including dividends), while the bottom chart represents price return (excluding dividends).

Middlesex Water's total return, including dividends, has been 1,701% over the past 30 years, while the company's price return (excluding dividends) has been just 538%. Dividends have therefore provided approximately 2/3 of the company's long-term return. Without dividends, MSEX would have underperformed the SP500 index over the past 30 years. Thanks to consistently growing dividends, its cumulative total return has been approximately 500% higher than the SP500.

The company's current dividend payout ratio of 55.81% is safe, being roughly the same as the 10-year average of 55.62%. Safety is also confirmed by the fact that the current payout ratio is lower than the sector median, which is 85.67%. The following chart provides an overview of MSEX's dividend payout ratio over the last decade.

Let's take a closer look at MSEX's dividend history over the past 10 years.

Year 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 TTM
Dividends 0.77 0.81 0.85 0.90 0.98 1.05 1.10 1.18 1.26 1.33 1.37

MSEX dividends 2015-2025 seekingalpha.com

MSEX has increased its dividend by 4-8 cents every year for the past decade. The average annual growth rate of dividends has been 5.93%. This is roughly the same growth rate as other water service companies. We are likely to see similar dividend growth in the years to come.

Financial Health

The following chart shows the development of MSEX's total liabilities and equity over the past 10 years.

Both total liabilities and company equity have been growing at a fairly similar pace over the last decade. The current debt/equity ratio of 1.84 is close to the decade average. The debt/equity ratio over the last decade has remained in the range of 1.66-2.19.A relatively high debt-to-equity ratio is typical of most water services companies. In my opinion the current debt/equity ratio of 1.84 does not indicate a weakening of the company's financial health.

MSEX's current interest coverage ratio of 4.43 is also completely safe. The company's current TTM EBIT is $59.9 million and total interest expense is $13.5 million. However, it should be mentioned as a weakness in financial health that the company's operating cash flow of $65.9 million represents only 18.75% of its long-term debt of $351.3 million.

Comparison with Peers

In terms of 5-year revenue growth rate, MSEX remains at the average level among peer companies.

Company MSEX CWCO YORW ARTNA GWRS CSZI
Revenue 5 year CAGR 7.60% 12.03% 7.57% 5.38% 8.27% 91.35%

5-year revenue CAGR of water service companies seekingalpha.com

Excluding Cadiz Inc, which has exceptional revenue growth, the 5-year revenue CAGR for the peer group is 8.17%. MSEX's 7.60% is slightly below that.

One of Middlesex Water's strengths relative to its peer group is its return on equity. MSEX has this metric at 9.80%, while the comparison group average is 8.89%. Also, with its net profit margin of 22.05%, it exceeds the comparison group's average of 19.78%. However, York Water Company had the strongest net margin of 25.91% in the peer group. The table below provides an overview of the return on equity and net margins of the companies in the comparison group.

Company MSEX CWCO YORW ARTNA GWRS
ROE 9.80% 7.90% 8.63% 9.04% 9.11%
Net Profit Margin 22.05% 20.75% 25.91% 19.60% 10.63%

Return on equity and net margins of water service companies seekingalpha.com

Valuation

Since MSEX's free cash flow is currently negative -$13.28 million, I will use the relative valuation method. The number of common shares of the company has increased from 16.2 million to 17.9 million over the past decade. Since the 10.49% dilution of earnings per share distorts the objectivity of the P/E method, I will analyze based on the EV/EBITDA method.

Year 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 TTM
EV/EBITDA 10.32 14.09 14.25 18.94 23.6 25.27 38.82 24.42 22.57 16.24 15.2

EV/EBITDA ratio of MSEX 2015-2025 source: roic.ai, investing.com

The average EV/EBITDA over the last decade has been 20.33. The trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 15.2 is 25.25% lower than this. The fair price of MSEX based on this method could be approximately $68.

The EV/Sales ratio has also been on a downward trend since 2022, as indicated by the graph below.

Year 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 TTM
EV/Sales 4.86 6.41 6.25 7.36 10.40 11.0 16.41 11.48 9.63 7.20 6.95

MSEX EV/Sales 2015-2025 seekingalpha

The average EV/Sales ratio for the decade has been 8.90. The current EV/Sales ratio of 6.95 is 22% lower than that. Based on the 10-year average EV/Sales ratio, the fair value of MSEX stock should be $66.36.

The third measure of relative valuation I will use is the P/B ratio. The chart below shows the company's P/B ratio over the last decade.

Year 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 TTM
P/B ratio 2.19 3.12 2.93 3.52 3.42 3.67 5.49 3.74 2.85 2.15 2.17

MSEX P/B 2015-2025 seekingalpha

The average P/B ratio over the last decade has been 3.20. The current P/B ratio of 2.17 is 32.19% lower than this. Since the current MSEX Book value per share is $25.1, the fair price of the share based on this metric would be as much as $80.32. 3.20 X 25.1=80.32.

Based on all three relative valuation metrics, MSEX's fair value falls within the range of $66.36-$80.32. However, I think $80.32 is too optimistic a value, so remaining conservative, I give MSEX a fair value of $66.36 per share. The company's stock is currently trading 18.03% below its fair value.

Risks

Middlesex Water is a company with a stable and established business model, which in my opinion has quite few risks. However, there are some factors that could prevent a company's share price from reaching fair value. One risk factor I see is the FED delaying interest rate cuts. Since MSEX has a high debt load, typical of water service companies, loan interest rates significantly affect this company.

Another important risk factor is the company's ability to achieve the desired water service tariffs. Developments over the last few years have shown that raising water service prices has sometimes only been partially successful. Such developments reduce the company's ability to make profits.

The third risk is MSEX's increasing operating costs. These are related to meeting both PFAS and other water quality requirements. Failure to obtain sufficient compensation to cover these costs could negatively impact a company's profit margins.

In Conclusion

MSEX is a good dividend stock that has also become a value stock due to its favorable valuation at the moment. The company has been managed skillfully, resulting in improved profitability over the last decade. Since MSEX offers a potential return of approximately 20% over the next 12 months, including dividends, in my estimation, I give this company a Buy rating.

data.title: Tesla: Robotaxis And The Balance Sheet Take Center Stage
data.description:
Is Elon Musk all the way back?

It's been a transition from work at the Doge on Capitol Hill to being back to the industrial auto body stamping factories in Austin, Fremont and Sparks. I last covered Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) in June, just after the contents of the Big Beautiful Bill were released in conjunction with Musk leaving the White House for good and starting a public spat on X.com with President Trump. All was not well in the world of Tesla Motors, both the budget cuts that Musk had championed with Doge and the EV tax credits were on the way out as was Elon.

Tesla Q2 2025 earnings

Not pretty. The YoY column on the far right is negative for almost every metric except CAPEX and cash and equivalents. Those aren't always the best numbers to have going positive when all of the profitability numbers are going negative. That means the company is both diluting to raise cash and spending more on CAPEX at the same time that sales are falling. Free cash flow is now barely break even. The one positive was the GAAP gross margin line that got back up to 17.2% quarter over quarter. It was hovering in the 16% range for a couple of quarters. This was a company mind you in the not too distant past that was getting a 20+% gross margin and was the talk of the town.

Furthermore, I'm starting to believe that the fallout between President Trump and Musk is genuine. It wouldn't be the first time that Trump and a close ally had a falling out. With that being said, the liberal part of Musk's base may be recoverable with Musk's distancing himself from the President and even starting his own political party. The EV tax credits aren't coming back, so maybe Musk is giving up on the notion that he'll get any preferential treatment.

Tesla Q2 2025 earnings call

CFO Vaibhav Taneja joined Musk to present the investor deck and took questions after. I have listened to more than a few earnings calls, and this was the first call post leaving Washington, DC, for Musk. Everyone was enthusiastic to hear if Musk was ready to roll his sleeves up and get back to sleeping on the factory floor. Musk seemed to lack the usual enthusiasm we are used to and painted a rather bleak picture about near term financial projections as it relates to the primary business, which is already facing problems with overseas competition, and will now face a rescinding of the EV tax credit.

Where are you Optimus?

The deck released with earnings didn't mention Optimus [at least my command F search didn't dig it out]. The deck presentation on call didn't mention it either. Analysts asked in the Q&A, Musk gave a brief "ramping up", "getting ready", type response but didn't try to harp upon the topic much more than that. For a company that everyone is waiting for to be the robotics leader of the world, this call was rather lackluster.

Robo-taxis were the central theme of the call

This is the main focus of the company to regain profitability growth as the company seems to have realized that ramping up Optimus would be both super CAPEX intensive and have no immediate path to profitability. Both Musk and Taneja consistently reiterated they were being careful with the roll-out and safety first for AV taxis. With Waymo gaining steam and adding markets left and right, Tesla has their work cut out for them. Musk still thinks they can have the AV taxi story working by early next year and be a leader. They also think cars will deliver themselves by then autonomously post purchase.

Musk stated again he thought the bottleneck to their sales was a lack of consumer financial resources. He said if consumers could begin to lease their vehicles out, like an Airbnb, it would increase affordability for many as the income could help pay the car note. A nice thought, but the amount of regulatory hoops they'll have to get through until that point is reached at a scale large enough to both turn a profit and get overall sales numbers to tick up seems hard to imagine. Part of the bet about being aligned with Trump priorly was that there would be a massive regulation overhaul in their favor to jumpstart this operation.

Is the xAI hat-trick still in the bag?

The final play to come that could help spur investor confidence and give them a new beacon of hope would be a merging of xAI and Tesla.

Musk made it official that he will seek greater collaboration between three of his companies: Tesla, xAI, and Twitter, in the form of an investment into xAI by Tesla.

xAI needs a lot of money to keep running more and more power intensive models. Tesla has a lot of money on the balance sheet. To this point, the cash does not seem to have a real purpose other than a safety net. It could be an interesting use of cash should xAI get to a point where it might be able to turn a profit on a 1-2 year horizon. If Tesla merged too early, xAI might burn through too much of Tesla's cash and leave the other operations out to dry. For now Musk may just be trying to infuse some cash into xAI in exchange for some equity to be owned by Tesla but not a controlling stake.

I don't think an all out merger happens this year if at all. The caveat here being if Tesla's share price drops into the lower $200s or less, then I would not be surprised if at least rumors of a merger are floated to stimulate the shareholder base. It will be interesting to see how investors vote on an equity or profit sharing stake into xAI. That alone could create a new bull thesis for investors.

Tesla balance sheet

This is where the company continues to shine. The shareholders continue to pay up for the hope that Elon capitalizes on all things physical AI and allows the company to dilute them in the process. We can see that shares outstanding [purple], follow the blue line, cash and short-term investments, very closely up and to the right. There is no other auto that could ever get away with this. This is Tesla's ace in the hole, and this allows them to keep chasing cash burning ideas. As of late, most have been funded in part by free cash flow, but that may be changing with the company's misfortunes. The balance sheet is now at the center of the equation and the absolute most important factor to keep an eye on.

Risks

The risks are many here. The company seems to be pivoting away from some big ideas that burned a lot of cash. Mainly the semi truck and cyber truck. These were barely mentioned in the deck or call after being a big theme in years prior.

Even energy and storage saw a year over year decline which is becoming surprising. That business seemed to be a bright spot on most earnings reports as it was a completely different revenue stream from the autos. When Musk left the helm, there must have been some execution issues to let this segment slip. Some of this might be tariff related with much of the battery supply chain being China based through CATL. Still, if Musk was there all along, he might have found a creative way to get some orders through before the supply chain became muddled.

My final thoughts: TSLA stock is still a hold

You buy Tesla for two reasons, Musk being an amazing marshal of resources and the balance sheet that is second to none in the auto industry. I believe the shares have run too much and incorporated too many future stories that have yet to materialize.

If the company uses the cash incorrectly, it could be a disaster. This is a pivotal time where the company has to decide if they cut costs to fund the robo-taxi business by generating more free cash flow, or do they go all in with the balance sheet and make a product that can compete with Waymo. Tesla is still a hold in my opinion while the balance sheet still has a lot of cash.
